1: mî-yittēn rōʾšî mayim — "Oh that my head were a spring of water"; the optative-wish formula (mî yittēn, "who will give?" = "would that...") expresses Jeremiah's grief-wish. The weeping-prophet motif is central to his identity — his tears are not weakness but covenantal mourning. The MKT preserves "weep day and night for the slain of my dear people" — the word ḥălāl (slain, pierced) emphasizes violent death.
2: ûminnāhem ʾēlek — "so that I could leave my people and go away from them"; Jeremiah's second wish: to escape from the people he is condemned to love. Gō mallôn ʾōrḥîm (a lodging place for travelers) in the wilderness — the wish for anonymity and distance. The MKT preserves the tension: Jeremiah does not hate his people, but the weight of their sin is unbearable to the prophet who must live among them.
3: qāšĕtû ʾet-lĕšônām qĕšĕtām — "They have bent their tongue like a bow to shoot falsehood"; the tongue-as-weapon metaphor: lāšôn (tongue) bent like a qešet (bow), shooting šeqer (falsehood/lies). The MKT preserves the imagery: "grown powerful in the land but not by truth." Wĕlōʾ leʾĕmûnâ gābarû bāʾārets — they have prevailed in the land but not through faithfulness.
4: kol-ʾāḥ ʿāqōb yaʿqōb — "every brother is a schemer"; this is a pun on the name Jacob (yaʿăqōb, from ʿāqēb, heel) — Jacob the heel-grasper/supplanter. The MKT renders "every brother is a schemer" rather than "every brother will act like Jacob" because the wordplay requires a footnote to work in English; the MKT chose the semantic content over preserving the untranslatable pun. Ideally a reader would know the Jacob connection.
5: limmĕdû lĕšônām dabber šeqer — "They have trained their tongue to lie"; limmēd (train, teach) implies deliberate practice — lying has become a cultivated skill. The phrase "they exhaust themselves committing iniquity" (nilʾû bĕhašĕkīl) portrays sin as hard work that they devote serious effort to. The MKT preserves this irony: the energy that should go toward YHWH goes toward deliberate deception.
6: bĕtôk mirmâ yāšābû — "You live in the midst of deception"; YHWH addresses Jeremiah directly about the people. Mirmâ (deception, treachery) is the ambient environment — they inhabit deception the way one inhabits a house. "Through their deceit they refuse to know me, declares the LORD" — knowledge of YHWH and deception are mutually exclusive; the commitment to falsehood necessarily drives out knowledge of the God of truth.
7: tsāraptîm ûbĕḥantîm — "I will refine them and test them"; the metallurgical purification metaphor: tsārap (smelt, refine) and bāḥan (test, assay) are the verbs for removing dross from metal. The rhetorical question "what else can I do with my sinful people?" (ʾêk ʾeʿĕśeh mippĕnê bat-ʿammî) conveys divine constrained purpose: the refining is YHWH's response to apostasy that cannot be ignored.
8: ḥēts šāḥûṭ lĕšônām — "Their tongue is a lethal arrow that shoots out deceit"; the tongue-bow metaphor of Jeremiah 9:3 is here intensified: the tongue is itself the arrow (ḥēts šāḥûṭ, a polished/sharpened arrow). The MKT renders "with their mouths they speak peace to their neighbors, but in their hearts they set a trap." The gap between spoken peace (šālôm) and planned ambush is the essence of social duplicity.
9: The covenant-lawsuit formula: "Should I not punish them for all this? Should I not take vengeance on such a nation?" The rhetorical double question (hăʾal-ʾēlleh lōʾ-ʾepqōd) expects the answer yes. Pāqad (punish, visit, attend to) is YHWH's covenant-enforcement verb — the same root used in promises of blessing. The MKT preserves both questions to maintain the juridical deliberation.
10: qînâ ûnĕhî — "weeping and wailing" and "lamentation" (nĕhî); the mourning vocabulary for the desolated mountains. The divine lament ("I will take up weeping" — YHWH is the subject) shows the peculiar Jeremianic characteristic: YHWH himself mourns for the land he is judging. The MKT preserves YHWH as the grieving subject to maintain this theological tension.
11: wĕnātattî ʾet-yĕrûšālaim lĕʿiyyîm — "I will make Jerusalem a heap of rubble"; ʿî (rubble-mound, desolate ruin) — the word used for the remnant-mounds of destroyed cities. "A haunt of jackals" (mĕʿôn tannîm): jackals inhabit ruins in OT imagery. The MKT preserves both images — rubble-mound and jackal-haunt — as the two paired symbols of total desolation.
12: A wisdom-challenge: "Who is wise enough to understand this? Who has received the word from the LORD and can explain it?" The two questions distinguish human wisdom from prophetic revelation. The MKT preserves the distinction: understanding (bîn) vs. having received the word (ʾăšer dibbartî YHWH ʾēlāyw). The answer (from YHWH in Jeremiah 9:13-16) requires the latter, not the former.
13: YHWH's answer to the wisdom-challenge: they abandoned his Torah. ʿāzĕbû ʾet-tôrātî — "they have abandoned my law that I placed before them"; nātan lĕpānêhem (placed before them) — the law was openly displayed/available, not hidden. The MKT follows MT in rendering Torah as "law" — the full Mosaic instruction-set that they comprehensively abandoned.
14: ʾaḥărê haShĕbāʿalîm — "went after the Baals"; the plural bĕʿālîm denotes the various local Baal-manifestations worshipped at different shrines. "Just as their ancestors had taught them" (kĕʾăšer limmĕdûm ʾăbôtām) — the apostasy is generationally transmitted; it is not mere individual deviation but a family tradition of idolatry. The MKT preserves the plural Baals and the ancestral transmission.
15: laʿănâ — "wormwood"; a bitter plant (Artemisia) used as the OT's metaphor for bitter suffering (Lamentations 3:15, 19). "Poisoned water" (mê rōʾš) — rōʾš is a poisonous plant (likely hemlock or a similar herb). The combination of bitter food and poisoned drink represents total inversion of covenant blessing (land of milk and honey). The MKT follows standard rendering of these paired covenant-curse images.
16: ûbizzîtîm bĕtôk haggôyim — "I will scatter them among nations"; zārâ (scatter, winnow — the agricultural image of grain chaff blown away) is the diaspora verb. The pursuit-by-sword until destroyed is the military covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:41-65). The MKT preserves the diaspora as divine act: "I will scatter... I will pursue."
17: hammĕqônĕnôt — "the professional mourning women"; qînâ (lament, dirge) + participle = women skilled in the art of public mourning. Professional mourners were a recognized occupational class in the ancient world. The MKT renders "professional mourning women" to clarify this was not amateur grief but skilled lament-performance. "The most skilled" (hāḥăkāmôt) — even the mourning requires expertise.
18: The professional mourners' commission: weep until eyes stream (tērednā dimʿâ ʿênênû) and eyelids overflow (yizzĕlû mayim ʿapʿappênû). The two different Hebrew words for eyes (ʿêyin, outer eye; ʿapʿappe, eyelid) indicate the totality of weeping. The MKT preserves both anatomical terms: "eyes... eyelids" — the total-body weeping for the total judgment.
19: The lament-cry from Zion: šuddadnû bōšnû meʾōd kî ʾāzabnû ʾārets — "We are devastated! We are utterly disgraced — because we have had to leave the land." The triple emphasis (devastated, disgraced, exiled) is the MT's way of conveying the magnitude of the loss. The MKT preserves all three expressions rather than collapsing them.
20: YHWH addresses the women directly: šimʿā nāšîm dĕbar YHWH — "women, hear the word of the LORD." The commission to teach daughters to mourn suggests the lament will outlast the immediate generation — it is something that must be institutionalized. The MKT preserves the direct female address as significant: the mourning women are given the prophetic commission to transmit the lament.
21: māwet ʿālâ bĕḥallônênû — "death has climbed through our windows"; Death is personified (māwet as a figure, possibly an echo of the Canaanite death-god Mot) entering houses through windows. "Cutting down children in the streets and young men in the public squares" — the harvest-metaphor for mass death: likrôt ʿôlāl meḥûts. The MKT preserves the personification of Death as an active agent.
22: wĕnāpal niblat hāʾādām kĕdōmen ʿal-pĕnê haśśādeh — "Human corpses will fall like manure on the open field"; the covenant-curse of unburied dead (Deuteronomy 28:26) in its starkest form. Dōmen (dung, manure) is a deliberately undignified comparison. The MKT preserves the bluntness — translation that softens "like manure" undermines the prophetic force.
23: ʾal-yithalēl ḥākām bĕḥokmātô — "Let not the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their strength, nor the rich in their riches"; the three-fold anti-boast formula. Hālal (boast, praise) — the same root as hallel. The wisdom/strength/riches triad represents the three spheres of human self-sufficiency. The MKT preserves all three in parallel because their cumulative effect is essential — no domain of human competence is exempt from this rejection.
24: kî ʾim bĕzōʾt yithalēl hammishtallēaḥ haskēl wĕyādōaʿ ʾôtî — "let him who boasts boast in this: that he understands and knows me"; the only legitimate boast is YHWH-knowledge (daʿat YHWH). The three divine attributes: ḥesed (steadfast love/covenant loyalty), mišpāṭ (justice), ṣĕdāqâ (righteousness) — the same triad that defines YHWH's character throughout the OT. Paul cites this verse in 1 Corinthians 1:31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17: "let him who boasts, boast in the Lord." The MKT's rendering of the three attributes is deliberate: "steadfast love, justice, and righteousness in the earth."
25: kol-mûl bĕʿorlâ — "all who are only outwardly circumcised"; the category of the physically circumcised who are simultaneously uncircumcised (i.e., in heart). The MKT renders "all who are only outwardly circumcised" — paraphrasing the literally ambiguous kol-mûl bĕʿorlâ (all circumcised in/with foreskin). This verse sets up the spiritual-circumcision contrast with Paul's argument in Romans 2:28-29.
26: ʿărēl lēb — "uncircumcised in heart"; the phrase used for the nations and equally for Israel. The MKT renders "for all these nations are uncircumcised in the flesh, and all the house of Israel is uncircumcised in heart." The startling equivalence: Israel's heart-circumcision deficit is no better than Gentile physical uncircumcision. This verse is the OT basis for Paul's argument in Romans 2:28-29 (circumcision of the heart, by the Spirit).
